title: [WIP]change macos directory for machine sockets
body:
if the machine name is long and/or the username is long, it is possible to exceed the 104byte limit for filenames in MacOS.  This PR shortcuts things to $HOME/.podman instead.

Fixes: #13609

[NO NEW TESTS NEEDED]

Signed-off-by: Brent Baude <bbaude@redhat.com>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]".  That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name.  Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

comments.body:
is there a way to know where podman is storing machine sockets ? as it's looking like a breaking change for clients assuming the location of the current podman socket.

From clients wanted to connect to this socket, is there a way to know the path by asking `podman` ?

for example `podman machine list --format json` does not contain the link to the `podman.sock` file

> is there a way to know where podman is storing machine sockets ? as it's looking like a breaking change for clients assuming the location of the current podman socket.
> 
> From clients wanted to connect to this socket, is there a way to know the path by asking `podman` ?
> 
> for example `podman machine list --format json` does not contain the link to the `podman.sock` file

it is a good question, and largely why I put a WIP on the PR.  In the end, I may need to use symlinks, but I really fear the maintenance of those.  I should also consider putting a cap on the length of the machine names.  We can control those but we cannot control the length of the user names.  So yeah, this fixes the issues and maybe creates some hard questions ....

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *baude* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [baude] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

I think it's fine to change the location as long as there is a way to grab the link to the socket from the `podman machine list --format json` data

like: 
```json
   {
        "Name": "podman-machine-default",
        "Default": true,
        "CPUs": 1,
        
        "HostSocket": "/path/to/podman.sock",
        
}
```

Also wondering why if there is only one socket available at a time it could not be in `/var/run/` like `docker.sock`

`/var/run/podman.sock` so it's independent of the user name for tooling

`/var/run/podman.sock` being a symlink to `$HOME/.podman/run/podman.sock`

> Also wondering why if there is only one socket available at a time it could not be in `/var/run/` like `docker.sock`
> 
> `/var/run/podman.sock` so it's independent of the user name

The one at a time is a VM backend limitation that hopefully goes away one day, as everything else is designed to support multiple machines per user, and multiple users. 

Writing to var requires root perms, so there is a helper agent that establishes a link for the /var/run/docker.sock support. A similar link could also be established for podman.sock, however, the main convention for podman is ssh urls using podman system connection, so nothing knows to look for it there.

> `/var/run/podman.sock` being a symlink to `$HOME/.podman/run/podman.sock`

Yeah, that's doable. The current commits here are using $HOME/.podman/podman.sock which is a link to the local machine sock. the helper could link podman.sock as well as docker.sock. It has to be installed though for it to be effective.
BTW the last one I just pushed has compatibility links for old clients and old machines.
Closing in favor of larger refactor do this "right" ->  #13674
